28: amēn lego hymin hoti panta aphethesetai tois huiois ton anthropon ta hamartemata kai hai blasphemiai — all sins will be forgiven the sons of men and whatever blasphemies they utter — the universality of forgiveness is stated first; then the single exception is given. blasphemia eis to pneuma to hagion — blasphemy against the Holy Spirit — the attribution of the Spirit's works (exorcism by the Spirit of God) to the demonic; the sin that cannot be forgiven because it involves the willful inversion of the Spirit's identification of Christ, thereby placing oneself permanently outside the Spirit's reach who grants repentance.
1: Eiselthen eis ten synagOgen, kai En ekei anthrOpos exErammenen echOn ten cheira — he entered again into the synagogue, and there was a man who had a withered hand — exErammenE (withered, dried up — from xerainO, to dry out) is the Greek medical term for a limb that has lost function through atrophy or nerve damage. The synagogue setting repeats the pattern of Mark 1:21-28: the place of Torah instruction becomes the arena of messianic authority. The unnamed man's condition is publicly visible, making the Sabbath dilemma equally public.
2: Kai paretEroun auton ei tois sabbasin therapeusei auton, hina katEgorEsOsin autou — and they were watching him closely to see if he would heal him on the Sabbath, in order that they might accuse him — paretEroun (imperfect of paratEreO, to watch beside, observe closely, lie in wait) is legal surveillance: they are gathering evidence for a formal accusation (katEgorEsOsin, legal charge). The purpose clause (hina + subjunctive) makes the intent explicit: the healing they are about to witness is pre-interpreted as potential grounds for prosecution. The Sabbath healing is being set up as a legal trap.
3: Kai legei tO anthrOpO tO exErammenen ten cheira — and he said to the man who had the withered hand — Jesus addresses the man directly before resolving the legal question, staging the healing as a public event (egeire eis to meson = rise into the middle). The command to stand in the center (meson) removes the man from obscurity and makes the healing fully visible to everyone in the synagogue. There will be no ambiguity about what happens or who does it.
4: Exestin tois sabbasin agathon poiesai E kakOpoiesai, psychen sOsai E apokteinai? — is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do evil, to save life or to kill? — the antithesis is sharp: doing good vs. doing evil, saving life vs. killing. Jesus frames the question so that refusing to heal is itself a moral act — inaction on behalf of suffering is not a neutral Sabbath posture but a form of evil. Hoi de esiOpOn — but they remained silent — the silence is not reverence but legal strategy: answering either way is a trap. Their silence confirms that they have no principled answer; they are not engaged in genuine halakhic debate but in evidence-gathering.
5: Kai periblepsamenos autous met' orgEs, syllypUmenos epi te pOrOsei tes kardias autOn — and looking around at them with anger, grieved at the hardness of their hearts — the dual emotional state is unique in the Gospels: orge (anger, righteous fury — same word as Isaiah 66:15; Romans 1:18 for divine wrath) and syllypoumenos (co-grieved, deeply distressed — the syn prefix adds the communal dimension: he shares in their suffering). Jesus is angry at the hardness and simultaneously grieved by it. The heart-hardness (pOrOsis tes kardias — pOrOsis = petrification, callusing) is the same condition described in Mark 6:52 (disciples after the feeding) and Mark 8:17 — a recurring Markan diagnosis.
6: Kai exelthontes hoi PharisaiOi euthys meta tOn HErOdianOn symBoulion edidoun kat' autou hopOs auton apolEsOsin — and the Pharisees immediately went out and took counsel with the Herodians against him, how they might destroy him — euthys; the immediate consultation with the Herodians is surprising: the Pharisees (a purity-observance movement) and the Herodians (a political party supporting the Herodian dynasty) had conflicting agendas and mutual suspicion. Their alliance against Jesus is the political equivalent of the Sadducee-Pharisee alliance in Mark 12:13-17: opposition to Jesus unites otherwise incompatible groups. ApolEsOsin (destroy) — this is the earliest death-plot in Mark, provoked by a healing.
7: Kai ho IEsous meta tOn mathEtOn autou anechOrEsen pros ten thalassan — and Jesus with his disciples withdrew toward the sea — anechOrEsen (withdrew, retreated) is the response to the death-plot of Mark 3:6: strategic withdrawal rather than direct confrontation. The movement toward the lake creates the outdoor teaching space of Mark 4:1-2. Kai poly plEthos apo tes Galilaias EkolouthEsen — and a great multitude from Galilee followed — the following crowd is pan-regional: Galilee (Mark 3:7), Judea, Jerusalem, Idumea, Transjordan, Tyre and Sidon (Mark 3:8). The geographic spread is Markan staging for the universal scope of the mission.
8: Kai apo Ierosolymön kai apo tes Idoumaias kai peran tou Iordanou kai peri Tyron kai Sidona — and from Jerusalem and from Idumea and beyond the Jordan and around Tyre and Sidon — the geographic list deliberately spans Jewish and Gentile territory: Idumea (south of Judea, the homeland of the Herodians), Transjordan (east), and Tyre/Sidon (Phoenician cities of the northwest coast, Gentile territory). The anticipation of the Gentile mission is embedded in the crowd geography. AkOontes hosa poiei Elthan pros auton — hearing all he was doing, they came to him — fame (akouO = hearing) as the medium of approach, consistent with the akoe pattern of Mark 1:28.
9: Kai eipen tois mathEtais autou hina ploiarion proskarte autO dia ton ochlon — and he told his disciples that a small boat should be ready for him because of the crowd — ploiarion (small boat, diminutive of ploion) is prepared as an escape valve: the crowd pressure was becoming physical (Mark 3:10). The boat enables Jesus to continue teaching at the lake without being overwhelmed (cf. 4:1 where the boat becomes the teaching platform). Hina mE thlibOsin auton — in order that they would not press him — thlibO (to press, crush, afflict) describes the physical crush of the crowd. Mark's vivid realism: the healer was in danger of being physically overwhelmed by those seeking healing.
10: Pollous gar etherapeuse, hOste epipiptein autO hina autou hapsOntai hosoi eichon mastigas — for he healed many, so that those who had diseases were pressing upon him in order to touch him — epipiptO (to fall upon, press heavily upon) describes the crowd's physical aggression: those with mastigas (scourges, plagues, afflictions — literally "whips," the word for the physical blows of a whipping; used metaphorically for illness) were pressing themselves against him. The connection between healing and touch is distinctive to Mark's physicality: the healings in Mark 5 (woman with hemorrhage, Mark 5:28-30; Jairus's daughter, Mark 5:41) both involve touch. The crowd's instinct to touch him is not wrong.
11: Kai ta pneumata ta akatharta, hotan auton etheOroun, prosEpipton autO kai ekrazon legontes hoti Sy ei ho hyios tou theou — and the unclean spirits, whenever they saw him, fell before him and cried out saying "you are the Son of God" — the hotan + imperfect construction indicates recurring behavior: every encounter with unclean spirits produced the same response. ProsEpipton (fell before, prostrated themselves — the posture of submission before a superior power) + ekrazon (they cried out). The demonic recognition and involuntary submission is the third instance of the Messianic Secret pattern. The title ho hyios tou theou (the Son of God) is identical to the baptismal declaration of Mark 1:11; demons know what disciples do not yet understand.
12: Kai polla epetima autois hina mE auton phaneron poiEsOsin — and he gave them many strict orders not to make him known/manifest — epitimaO (to charge/rebuke strictly, command with authority) + phaneron poiEsOsin (to make manifest, make publicly known). The Messianic Secret command here is the most explicit: the demons are ordered not to reveal him. The word phaneros (manifest, visible, publicly disclosed) is the disclosure term Mark uses at 4:22 for what will ultimately be revealed; the secret suppression is not permanent but a matter of timing and proper channel.
13: Kai anabainei eis to oros kai proskAleitai hous Ethelen autos — and he went up onto the mountain and summoned those whom he himself wanted — the ascent of the mountain is a Sinai-echo: the law-giver ascended the mountain; now the one with greater authority constitutes a new community. Hous Ethelen autos (those whom he himself wanted) — the sovereign, personal choice (autos emphatic) is the basis of the calling: they are chosen, not volunteers. Kai apElthon pros auton — and they came to him — the response matches the sovereign call: no resistance, no deliberation mentioned.
14: Kai epoiEsen dOdeka hina Osin met' autou — and he appointed/made twelve so that they might be with him — the founding purpose of the Twelve is stated first: hina Osin met' autou (in order that they might be with him) — the primary calling is not to do but to be with. The second and third purposes follow: kai hina apostellE autous kEryssein (to send them out to preach) and kai echein exousian ekballein ta daimonia (to have authority to cast out demons). The ordering matters: being with Jesus precedes and grounds the sending; presence is the source of the commission's authority.
15: Kai echein exousian ekballein ta daimonia — and to have authority to cast out demons — the three purposes of the Twelve appointment (Mark 3:14-15) match the three activities of Jesus's own ministry pattern: presence/relationship (Mark 3:14a), preaching (Mark 3:14b), exorcism (Mark 3:15). The Twelve are constituted as extensions of Jesus's own mission, carrying the same authority (exousia) that distinguished his teaching (Mark 1:22) and his commands to unclean spirits (Mark 1:27). The commissioning is a delegation of the Christological authority, not a separate source of power.
16: Kai epoiEsen tous dOdeka, kai epethEken onoma tO SimOni Petron — and he appointed the twelve, and he gave Simon the name Peter — epethEken onoma (placed/bestowed a name upon) — the act of naming carries authority: in the OT, God renames Abram/Abraham, Jacob/Israel; the one who names exercises sovereignty over the named. Petros (Peter) is the Greek transliteration of the Aramaic Kēphā (Cephas, rock/stone, 1 Corinthians 1:12; Galatians 1:18). The renaming precedes the full commissioning of Peter's apostolic role; it is a prophetic act whose full significance becomes clear only in retrospect.
17: Kai Iakōbon ton tou Zebedaiou kai IOannEn ton adelphon tou Iakōbou, kai epethEken autois onomata BoanErges, ho estin huioi brontEs — and James the son of Zebedee and John the brother of James, and he gave them the name Boanerges, which is "sons of thunder" — BoanErges is a Greek transliteration of an Aramaic expression (possibly bney ragsha or bney re'em); Mark provides the Greek translation (huioi brontEs = sons of thunder). The name is preserved only in Mark and is not used elsewhere in the NT. The reason for the nickname is never explained; retrospectively, it may reflect the temperament displayed in Luke 9:54 (calling down fire) or the ambition of Mark 10:37 (requesting seats of honor). Mark uniquely preserves the Aramaic nickname alongside its translation.
18: Kai Andrean kai Philippon kai BartholomaIon kai Matthaion kai ThOman kai Iakōbon ton tou Alphaiou kai Thaddalon kai SimOna ton KananaIon — and Andrew and Philip and Bartholomew and Matthew and Thomas and James the son of Alphaeus and Thaddaeus and Simon the Cananaean — the list of apostles (cf. Matthew 10:2-4; Luke 6:14-16; Acts 1:13) varies in ordering but shares the core Twelve. KananaIos (the Cananaean) is not "from Cana" but the Aramaic equivalent of the Greek ZElOtEs (Zealot, Luke 6:15; Acts 1:13), likely indicating association with or origin in a zealot movement. The list includes a tax collector (Levi/Matthew, Mark 3:14 of Mark 2) and a zealot — political opposites in one apostolic community.
19: Kai Ioudan Iskariōth, hos kai paredōken auton — and Judas Iscariot, who also betrayed him — the final name on the list is the only one with an appended identification, and that identification is hos kai paredōken auton (who also betrayed him). The kai (also) is pointed: Judas was appointed to be with Jesus and to preach and to cast out demons — and he also betrayed him. The betrayal is noted at the moment of the appointment, not as an afterthought. Iskariōth may derive from ish-Keriyot (man of Kerioth, a Judean town), identifying Judas as the only non-Galilean among the Twelve.
20: Kai erchontai eis oikon, kai synerchetai palin ochlos, hOste mE dynasthai autous mEde arton phagein — and they entered a house, and the crowd came together again, so that they could not even eat bread — the domestic retreat is immediately overwhelmed. Mēde arton phagein (not even to eat bread) is a vivid detail of the extreme demand on Jesus: even the minimum physical sustenance is impossible. This detail recurs at Mark 6:31 (oudE phagein eukairoun). The crowds' need is so constant that the normal rhythms of eating are disrupted. Mark's realism here — the press of the crowds does not allow even meals — gives the ministry a physical exhaustion dimension.
21: Kai akousantes hoi par' autou exElthon kratEsai auton, elegon gar hoti exestE — and those beside him, having heard, went out to seize him; for they said, "he is beside himself" — hoi par' autou (those beside him, those of his household) almost certainly refers to Jesus's family, confirmed by Mark 3:31's reference to his mother and brothers. KratEsai (to seize, take hold of by force — the same verb used for arresting Jesus in Mark 14:1,44,46,49) is forceful: they intended to physically remove him. ExestE (he has gone out of himself, he is beside himself, out of his mind) — the family's diagnosis is that Jesus has lost his sanity; the religious authorities' diagnosis (Mark 3:22) is that he is demonized. Both interpretations of Jesus's behavior are rejected by the narrative.
22: Kai hoi grammateis hoi apo Ierosolymōn katabanteEs elegon hoti BeelZeboul echei kai hoti en tO archonti tOn daimonion ekballei ta daimonia — and the scribes who came down from Jerusalem were saying "he has Beelzebul" and "by the ruler of demons he casts out demons" — the Jerusalem delegation arriving in Galilee is significant: this is not a local Pharisaic grumbling but a formal evaluation sent from the religious capital. BeelZeboul (various spellings; possibly ba'al-zebul = lord of the high dwelling or lord of flies/filth, cf. 2 Kings 1:2-3) is identified as the prince/ruler of demons (archonti tOn daimonion). The charge is the mirror-inverse of the truth: Jesus casts out demons by the Spirit of God (Matthew 12:28); they attribute the same act to the demonic ruler.
23: Kai proskalesamenos autous en parabolais elegen autois, POs dynatai Satanas Satanan ekballein? — and having called them to him, he began to speak to them in parables: "how can Satan cast out Satan?" — proskalesamenos autous (having called them to him) — Jesus initiates the debate by drawing his accusers into proximity; he does not avoid the charge but responds directly. The parabolic counter-argument begins with an internal-coherence question: an exorcist who serves Satan would be self-defeating. Satanas Satanan ekballein — Satan casting out Satan — the logical contradiction of the accusation is stated in the most compressed form: the premise makes the action structurally impossible.
24: Kai ean basileia eph' heautEn meristhE, ou dynatai stathEnai hE basileia ekeinE — and if a kingdom is divided against itself, that kingdom cannot stand — the argument from analogy: a divided kingdom is structurally self-defeating. Meristhē (divided, distributed, parceled out — from merizO) applied to a kingdom: a realm at war with itself cannot sustain its own coherence. The application to the exorcism charge: if Jesus's exorcisms are Satanic acts, Satan is destroying his own domain. The internal coherence of the demonic realm (however disordered) would be undermined by casting out its own members.
25: Kai ean oikia eph' heautEn meristhE, ou dynEsetai hE oikia ekeinE stathEnai — and if a house is divided against itself, that house cannot stand — the household (smaller than a kingdom) provides the second analogical level: domestic division produces structural collapse. The household image is immediate and observable; every hearer knows that a family that fights against itself destroys itself. The double analogy (kingdom, Mark 3:24; household, Mark 3:25) establishes the principle at both scales before the application to the demonic realm in Mark 3:26.
26: Kai ei ho Satanas anestE eph' heauton kai emeristhE, ou dynatai stathEnai alla telos echei — and if Satan has risen against himself and is divided, he cannot stand but has come to an end — the application of the analogy to Satan directly: if the exorcisms represent Satan acting against himself, it means his end (telos echei = has an end, is finished). The Christological implication: that Satan's kingdom is in fact falling is correct, but the reason is not internal division but the arrival of the stronger one (cf. Mark 1:7: ho ischyroteros mou) who binds him from outside.
27: All' ou dynatai oudeis eis ten oikian tou ischyrou eiselthOn ta skeue autou diarpasai, ean mE prOton ton ischyron dEsE kai tote ten oikian autou diarpasei — but no one can enter the house of the strong man and plunder his goods unless first he binds the strong man; and then he will plunder his house — the strong-man parable is the Christological key to the entire Beelzebul controversy. HO ischyros (the strong man) = Satan; Jesus is the one who has first bound him (prOton ton ischyron dEsE), making the exorcisms possible. The exorcisms are not signs of demonic alliance but signs of demonic defeat: Jesus plunders the strong man's house because he has already bound the strong man. The binding may refer to the wilderness temptation (Mark 1:13) or to the eschatological power of the kingdom.
29: Hos d' an blasphEmEsE eis to pneuma to hagion, ouk echei aphesin eis ton aiOna, alla enochos estin aiOniou hamartEmatos — but whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit does not have forgiveness unto the age, but is guilty of an eternal sin — aiOniou hamartEmatos (eternal sin/offense — aiOnios = of the age, pertaining to the coming age, age-long) is unique in the NT. The standard translation "unforgivable sin" accurately captures the practical meaning (ouk echei aphesin eis ton aiOna = does not have forgiveness for the age) but the term itself is eternal sin, emphasizing its age-to-come consequences. The sin is the attribution of the Holy Spirit's works (exorcism) to the demonic — a permanent inversion that places oneself outside the Spirit's reach who would grant repentance.
30: Hoti elegon, Pneuma akatharton echei — because they were saying "he has an unclean spirit" — Mark 3:30 is Mark's editorial explanation of why Jesus spoke the unforgivable-sin saying: it was a direct response to the specific charge that he was possessed by an unclean spirit. The explanation clarifies the referent of "blasphemy against the Holy Spirit": it is specifically the inversion of Spirit-empowerment and demonic possession — claiming that the Holy Spirit's exorcistic work is instead the work of an unclean spirit. This is not a general category of sin but a very specific theological inversion.
31: Kai erchontai hE mEtEr autou kai hoi adelphoi autou kai exO stEkontes apesteilan pros auton kalountes auton — and his mother and his brothers came and standing outside they sent word to him, calling him — the family arrives (cf. Mark 3:21 where they went out to seize him). HExO stEkontes (standing outside) is spatially significant: Jesus's physical family is outside the house; the crowd is inside. The narrative has already set up the redefinition of family in Mark 3:33-35. Hoi adelphoi autou (his brothers) — the natural reading is biological siblings; the alternative (cousins, or children of Joseph from a previous marriage) is theologically motivated but grammatically strained.
32: Kai ekatheto peri auton ochlos, kai legousin autO, Idou hE mEtEr sou kai hoi adelphoi sou exO zEtousin se — and a crowd was sitting around him, and they said to him: "look, your mother and your brothers are outside seeking you" — ekatheto peri auton (was sitting in a circle around him) — the crowd physically surrounds Jesus in the posture of disciples receiving teaching. This spatial arrangement will become the answer to the question in Mark 3:33: the circle around him is the definition of his family. ZEtousin se (seeking you) — the same verb used in Mark 1:37 (everyone seeks you!) and in 3:32 and will be used of those who seek Jesus's death. The usage here is neutral but carries the ambiguous charge of the word.
33: Kai apokritheis autois legei, Tis estin hE mEtEr mou E hoi adelphoi mou? — and answering them he said: "who is my mother and who are my brothers?" — the rhetorical question (tis estin = who is?) sets up the redefinition. The question is not a denial of biological relationship but a relativization of it: the biological bond, however real, is not the primary or ultimate category of kinship. Jesus will answer with a gesture (Mark 3:34) and a declarative statement (Mark 3:35) that redefines family on the basis of shared orientation toward the Father's will rather than biological descent.
34: Kai periblepsamenos tous peri auton kuklO kathEmenous legei, Ide hE mEtEr mou kai hoi adelphoi mou — and looking around at those sitting in a circle around him he said: "here are my mother and my brothers" — periblepsamenos (having looked around — the Markan characteristic scanning gaze, also at Mark 3:5; Mark 5:32; Mark 9:8; Mark 10:23) with kuklO (in a circle) — the same word as the crowd's seating arrangement in Mark 3:32. The gestural answer: Ide (Look! Behold!) pointing to the seated disciples. The ones gathered around him in the posture of receiving teaching are identified as his mother and brothers — a community of belonging constituted by presence with Jesus and attentiveness to his word.
35: Hos an poiEsE to thelEma tou theou, houtos adelphos mou kai adelphE kai mEtEr estin — whoever does the will of God, that one is my brother and sister and mother — the addition of adelphE (sister) to the male-focused terms brother and mother signals the inclusive scope of the new family. Poieo to thelEma tou theou (does the will of God) is the defining criterion: not biological descent, not ethnic identity, not religious observance as such, but active alignment with the Father's purpose. The same criterion appears in Matthew's Gospel in a different context (Mark 7:21; Mark 12:50); here it defines not entry into the kingdom but membership in the family of Jesus.
